data.title: Clean Energy Powers Back: Alt-Energy ETFs Surge In 2025 Comeback
data.description:
By James Picerno

Stocks in the alternative-energy endured a rough ride following the pandemic, but 2025 looks set to market a sharp rebound for this formerly battered industry.

After four straight calendar year losses, the tide appears to have turned for so-called clean-energy firms. A benchmark created by TMC Research that tracks the five biggest alt-energy ETFs is up nearly 37% year to date (through December 2). That’s a hefty return premium over this year’s rise for the US stock market, which is up 16.1% via the S&P 500 Index. Clean energy’s rally is even further ahead of conventional fossil fuel energy stocks, which has gained 7.8% year to date, based on the Energy Sector SPDR ETF (XLE), a proxy for Big Oil names such as Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX).

​The recovery in renewable energy stocks may strike some observers as surprising, given the policy changes in Washington this year that are widely seen as headwinds for the renewables industry. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which was signed into law in July, rolled back several key tax incentives that favored clean energy projects following the passage of the Inflation Reduction Act in 2022.

Yet, a combination of factors has sparked a rally this year. At the core of the bullish climate for these stocks: demand for energy is increasing, and renewables are increasingly part of the solution.

A Green Energy Tailwind

From an investment perspective, the selling in previous years may have gone too far and disconnected from the business prospects. As a result, the sector started 2025 with deeply discounted valuations. At one point in the year, stocks in this corner traded at a record discount of 67% relative to global stocks, according to analysis by Reuters.

While investor sentiment for green energy has been fickle, the business has been consistently bullish. Growth in renewable energy capacity continues to rise at a robust pace. Renewable energy is projected to soon surpass coal as the world’s leading source of electricity, if it hasn’t already, marking a historic shift in global power generation, according to the International Energy Agency (IEA).

Adding to energy demand is the surge in the rapid use of artificial intelligence (AI) data centers. The growth of AI is straining energy systems, with data centers and advanced chips among the fastest-rising electricity users. In the US, data centers could reach 8.6% of total power demand by 2035 - double today’s share, according recent IEA forecasts.

Reading the writing on the wall, several big-tech firms, such as Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN) and Google (GOOG, GOOGL), have announced billions of dollars for developing renewable energy projects. A similar trend is playing out globally as more firms target renewable energy projects. Bloomberg NEF, a data firm, estimates that global investment for new renewable energy sources reached a record $386 billion in the first half of this year.

While the One Big Beautiful Bill Act has created headwinds for clean energy, energy project developers have been adapting. Meanwhile, robust growth for energy has kept investment monies flowing into new projects.

Crux, a finance tech firm, reports [email registration required] that total project lending in the US clean energy industry rose $86 billion in the first half of 2025, up from $80 billion in the comparable period for 2024.

“The market continues to diversify, and, over the long term, a diverse array of technologies is expected to support sustained market volumes,” Crux notes. “The economics of clean energy investment are more compelling than ever, due to rising power demand and elevated electricity costs across the US.”

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: CenterPoint Energy: A Particularly Well-Positioned Utility
data.description:
Shares of CenterPoint Energy (CNP) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining about 20%. The company has benefited from improved operating performance following meaningful investments in resiliency and reliability. At the same time, growing optimism about potential electricity demand from data centers has lifted shares. I last covered shares in September when I upgraded CenterPoint to a “Buy,” and since then, CNP has returned about 3%, in line with the market. With updated financials and the 2026 outlook coming into clearer view, now is a good time to revisit CenterPoint.

CenterPoint now has a $65 billion 10-year capital plan. It sees a potential $10 billion of capacity investments beyond this. Currently, it expects the peak load in its main Houston Electric territory to grow by 50% over the next six years. Under its plan, it will invest about $7 billion a year in 2026-2028 and ~$6-$6.5 billion thereafter. As you can see, its Houston Electric division is getting the majority of its capital spending. In fact, Texas will be allocated about 70% of go-forward capital. This spending represents a meaningful step-up from the ~$5.5 billion this year.

Importantly, we are seeing CNP’s cap-ex lead to stronger results. In the company’s third quarter, reported on Oct. 23, CenterPoint Energy earned $0.50 per share, which beat expectations by $0.06. This beat was consistent with my view in September that CNP would generate more earnings than normal in H2 given the timing of its rate cases. While earnings are typically H1 loaded, I expected 2025 to be H2 weighted, which is playing out. I view this as just a 2025 phenomenon and expect the company to return to a more normal earnings seasonality next year. This beat helps reaffirm credibility in management after variations in its seasonal pattern caused H1 earnings misses, which now appear completely transitory.

We are seeing the benefits of its capital program and the associated rate recaptures. Earnings were up $0.19 from last year, with about $0.07 of tailwinds from growth and rate recovery. Operating & maintenance costs were also material benefits. As a reminder, this spending was unusually high last year as the company worked aggressively following tropical storm Beryl to improve resiliency, pulling forward necessary activities like trimming trees near lines, etc., after its initial response to the storm was widely seen as too slow.

We are seeing material demand growth across its system. Throughput on Houston Electric’s system is running up 9% this year, with industrial activity rising 17% in Q3. CNP is benefiting from the strong demographic trends in Texas. Plus, increased chemical and energy export activity is also providing industrial tailwinds that should be largely structural in nature. AI data centers are the focus of markets, of course, and this demand is still in its infancy and likely to support incremental electricity usage, which is why CNP anticipates material load growth over the next decade.

The primary challenge is generating enough capital to finance this cap-ex. CenterPoint’s FFO/debt ratio is 14%, up from 13.6% at the start of the year, consistent with its target. At this leverage, CNP can issue debt consistent with cash flow growth, but it cannot leverage its balance sheet further. Instead, the company needs to issue $2.75 billion of equity through 2030, the first billion of which it has locked in via forward sales. It does not expect to need to sell incremental equity until 2028. It will need to issue about $3 billion of equity from 2028 to 2035. Management is now targeting 11% rate base growth through 2030, but it is targeting 7%-9% EPS growth, reflecting the dilutive impact of a higher share count. Still, this is a healthy pace of earnings growth.

Additionally, alongside results, CenterPoint announced an agreement to sell its Ohio unit for a net $2.4 billion in proceeds, consistent with prior plans. The company has been selling off non-Texas assets, becoming more of a pure play in this faster-growth state. This sale also helps to fund its ambitious capital program while reducing the need to issue equity. It sold Ohio at 1.9x its rate base and 26x earnings, which I view as an attractive multiple, particularly given the fact Ohio is not as attractive of a territory as Houston. Generally speaking, all incremental capital will be funded with 47% equity. However, thanks to proceeds from the Ohio sale, this proportion may be a bit lower.

Frankly, the biggest risk to me facing CNP (and the utility sector as a whole) is not the financing needs, cap-ex projects, or even whether demand growth materializes, but rather the rise in electricity prices. As you can see below, after being stable across most of the 2010s, electricity prices (like many things) surged post-COVID, and they have continued to rise. There are multiple factors for this rise: general inflation, efficiency standards, higher natural gas prices (due in part to Russia’s invasion of Ukraine), and incremental data center demand.

The last reason, data center demand, has become a focus politically, with many consumers unhappy with the possibility they are paying higher prices to support AI data centers being built by large tech companies. With data center capex still ramping up, I expect this to be an increasing political focus. This is relevant because utilities seek to recoup investments they make in rate cases to their regulators. If electricity prices continue to rise and political pressure builds, recapturing investments may prove more difficult.

While this is an industrywide risk, I view CenterPoint as being relatively well positioned for it. As you can see below, monthly bills in Houston have risen far below peers and broader inflation. Efficient O&M spending and a growing customer base (allowing CNP to spread the same fixed costs across more customers) have enabled it to keep prices relatively low. This strong performance should provide more support from regulators for favorable rate case outcomes. It will be important for CNP to deliver on planned cost savings of 1%-2%/year and efficiencies to keep bill inflation low given this environment.

Alongside results, the company reiterated both 2025 guidance of $1.75-$1.77 and 2026 guidance of $1.89-$1.91. The 2025 outlook is consistent with my view. Back in September, I saw the company growing its rate base 8%-10% and EPS by 6.5%-8% through 2030 (with its dividend growing at the same pace). This drove my expectation for a long-term return in the 9%-10.5% range (dividend yield + dividend growth). Its capital plan is a bit more aggressive than I previously expected, resulting in a slightly higher EPS outlook.

Interestingly, management expects its rate base to grow 3% faster per year than EPS. This is a bit wider than the 2% I expected. This reflects the fact, in my view, that incremental cap-ex is less accretive to shareholders because CNP needs to issue a higher proportion of equity to support incremental spending, increasing the offsetting dilution. As such, even if it can execute on its other $10 billion of growth projects, I see limited benefit to shareholders. We are not yet at the point where incremental capital spending is negative for shareholders, but we are approaching a point of diminishing returns.

Now, CNP shares currently offer a 2.3% dividend yield. I view 7%-8% dividend growth as likely through 2030, creating a ~10% long-term return potential. Shares are trading at ~20x 2026 earnings, which is actually a discount to where it sold Ohio (26x). This is another reason why I view that sale as a wise capital allocation decision. Plus, it points to CNP shares having further upside. With a ~10% long-term potential, I view CNP as an attractive long-term investment, especially as its ability to keep customer inflation muted insulates it from the fastest-growing risk in the sector.

data.title: Why I Am Upgrading Wix.com
data.description:
I initiated coverage of Wix.com Ltd. (WIX) near the end of August with a Sell, and the stock has collapsed as I had suggested it might. I am upgrading the stock to Hold from Sell. In this article, I discuss their Q3, assess the outlook and take a look at the chart.

WIX Q3 Had Some Issues

My initiation piece on WIX came after they reported Q2, and I was concerned about several things. I discussed in that article that the financials have been challenged, that the valuation was too high and that the chart was breaking down. The stock did rally after my article, and the company did refinance its convertible note, issuing new convertible notes in September ($1 billion, with a 0% coupon, a five-year maturity and a conversion price of $210.49, a premium of 37.5% as of the day before pricing).

The company reported its Q3 on 11/19 before the market opened. Revenue of $505.2 million was a little bit higher than what had been expected and grew 14% from a year earlier and 3% sequentially. Bookings also rose 14%. Creative Subscriptions, which represented 70.5% of revenue, grew 12%, while Business Solutions at 29.5% showed 18% growth.

The company does not break out its AI sales. Base44 was acquired in June and the company expressed strong growth in market share there, suggesting that it will be on track to do $50 million in annual revenue by the end of Q4. This is a small percentage of overall revenue.

Non-GAAP net income per share was $1.68 per diluted share. Cash flow from operations was $128.7 million, leading to free cash flow of $127.3 million. Excluding acquisition-related costs, the company reported free cash flow of $159.4 million. The company once again spent a lot of money on share repurchases, paying $175 million for 1.3 million shares ($136.64 per share on average). In Q2, it had spent $100 million at an average price of $154.69. I continue to question the aggressive repurchasing.

I was concerned about the balance sheet, which showed equity of -$117.7 million at the end of Q2. Tangible equity was -$211.6 million. At the end of Q3, which included the debt refinancing and expansion, equity was -$278.8 million, with tangible equity at -$453 million. I have nothing against negative tangible value, especially for companies like WIX with such good cash flow, but it does make me question the company's aggressive repurchase of stock.

The Outlook Is Not Great

WIX has guided for full 2025 revenue of $1.99-2.00 billion. 21 analysts are projecting $2.00 billion, which would be a 13.4% gain. EPS are projected by a dozen analysts to grow 5% to $6.72. This margin pressure is due to investments being made in AI. Ahead of the report, analysts, according to Koyfin, had been expecting revenue in 2025 to be $1.99 billion with EPS of $6.81.

Looking at the outlook for 2026, analysts had been expecting revenue of $2.26 billion with EPS of $7.71. The current projection of $2.28 billion, a little higher, represents 14% expected growth for the year. EPS, though, has lower expectations, as analysts project $6.88, which would be up 2% (and not exciting). This looks like the reason for the large decline recently.

The WIX Chart has Plunged

I was concerned about the breakdown in the chart three months ago, and it has broken down worse:

As it did in May after Q1, it left a gap in November up to $123.90. If it gets filled, as the May gap was, it would be a move of +29%. The stock has potential support at $90, and the three-year low is near $70. The price is 35% below the 150-day moving average and 61% below the all-time high set earlier this year.

WIX is a leading website host but is also an AI company (Base44). Another large website hosting company is GoDaddy Inc. (GDDY), which has a larger market cap. GDDY has been beaten up, but not by as much as WIX. Here are the last three years:

WIX is now down 55% year-to-date and may be a tax-loss selling candidate. It has declined 46% in Q4 so far. The S&P 500 has gained 16.3% in price this year and is up 2% in Q4. Technology has done even better in 2025 and in Q4 I like the idea of buying stocks that are being sold for tax-loss reasons, and that may be the case here.

Conclusion

WIX has had a very disappointing year for its shareholders, falling tremendously despite growing its business and expanding into the hot AI sector. I think that the reaction to the Q3 report has been sharp and perhaps excessive, perhaps related to tax-loss selling. The PE based on the lowered 2026 outlook is now just 13.9X. The valuation seems okay to me, though I am concerned about Technology stocks, recommending the sale of large-cap Technology ETFs recently. For those who like WIX, it might be a good time to buy, but I will keep waiting. I am Neutral on the stock now.

data.title: Grail: Downgraded From Speculative Buy To Hold As The Stock Price Appreciated Faster Than Expected
data.description:
Introduction

When I published this article rating Grail Inc. (GRAL) as a Speculative Buy in late September 2025, the stock was trading at around $55 per share, and the company commanded a market capitalization of $2.4 billion. The company’s jaw-dropping $10 billion cumulative losses, as reported in its then-latest Form 10-Q aka quarterly report, probably drove away many potential investors. Its narrow stream of revenue from Galleri, its multi-cancer early detection (MCED) blood test, predominantly from self-paying patients, likely did not inspire investor confidence either.

I bought some shares in early September because my analysis led me to conclude that the stock market was underpricing the extraordinary long-term commercial potential of the so-called liquid biopsy industry in general and Galleri in particular.

Since then, to my pleasant surprise, the stock has climbed to around $100 today. I believe it is now appropriate and necessary to review the intervening developments that led to the stock’s dramatic rise.

Three Catalysts That More Than Doubled the Stock in Months

Although the stock price appreciation coincided with the overall improvement in sentiment toward biotech stocks, three major catalysts directly reshaped investor perceptions specific to Grail Inc.

The first catalyst came around October 15, 2025, when Samsung Electronics and Samsung C&T signed a binding letter of intent to collaborate with Grail on commercializing Galleri throughout Asia. Samsung also agreed to purchase $110 million of Grail stock at around $70 per share, representing a significant premium over the then-prevailing market price.

This news drove Grail’s shares up by no less than 15%. Mr. Market apparently understood the significance of having a prominent global conglomerate publicly affirming Galleri’s market potential with its own cash and credibility.

South Korea is an attractive market for Grail, and you cannot expect a better commercial partner there than Samsung. With a population of 52 million, a per-capita GDP above US$50,000, and about 20% of its population (about 10 million people) over age 65, it represents a meaningful market for Grail.

Equally importantly, Samsung—essentially the TSMC (a world-leading advanced chip designer and foundry) and the Apple of South Korea (with its smartphones dominating the South Korean market) combined—boasts unmatched marketing prowess and distribution channels to drive public awareness and physician adoption alike. With Samsung committing both its capital and its commercial infrastructure, Grail gains immediate credibility and the ground force for accelerating MCED screening adoption in one of Asia’s most affluent, aging populations.

Immediately thereafter, the company released PATHFINDER II clinical trial results, which provided further confirmation of Galleri’s clinical validity and value on top of PATHFINDER I. For more than 25,000 trial participants, the addition of Galleri to standard-of-care screening increased cancer detection by more than sevenfold. The test also maintained a specificity above 99% and a positive predictive value in the low-60% range.

These results represent a substantial step forward toward the goal of obtaining FDA approval for Galleri, and materially strengthened Grail’s case with regulators, payors, and clinicians by demonstrating Galleri's increasing prowess to detect cancers at early stages without overwhelming the healthcare system with excessive numbers of false positives.

The third development was perhaps the most dramatic: Abbott Laboratory’s (ABT) agreement to acquire Grail's competitor Exact Sciences (EXAS) for $21 billion on November 20, 2025. That news re-rated the valuation framework for the entire liquid biopsy industry, at least for the companies whose shares are publicly traded.

Even if comparing Cologuard and the related products against Galleri is like comparing apples and oranges, the price tag of Exact Sciences made one point clear: Big Pharma are willing to pay substantial sums for up-scalable cancer-screening blood tests if they have long-term potential for society wide adoption.

Grail’s Business Fundamentals Are Improving

These three developments explain why Grail’s market capitalization expanded from roughly $2.4 billion at the time of my first article to around $4.0 billion today. The company’s own execution also contributed meaningfully, or at least did not pull the stock price back.

In the most recent quarter, Grail generated slightly over $36 million of revenue, representing year-over-year growth in the mid-20s. The company sold more than 45,000 Galleri tests (once again, mostly self-paid) during the quarter, with cumulative tests surpassing 420,000, and gross margins expanding into the mid-50%. Net losses narrowed, and management now expects to cut cash burn in half for 2025.

In terms of its cash runway, Grail ended the quarter with more than half a billion dollars in cash and marketable securities. If the proposed partnership goes forward, Grail will soon add Samsung’s capital. These improvements, together with the company’s continued relationships with Quest, athenahealth, and others, indicate a business that is growing at a healthy clip.

Where the Bear Case Breaks Down

All of the above strengthens a bullish case. However, valuation must remain a central consideration for any investment decision, especially if it is a speculative investment to begin with as this one was.

When I made my purchase in early September, the market was grossly underpricing the long-term potential of Galleri. Some investors were anchoring to the microeconomics of company on the $949 price tag on Galleri, the cash-pay barriers, and the reimbursement cliff for Grail to climb over, without giving appropriate weight to how MCED testing would evolve once regulators, employers, and payors better understood its clinical value or once its costs declined through upscaling.

This is precisely the point I recently made to a bearish analyst who viewed Galleri’s economics through too static a lens. It is not realistic to assume that the long-run economics of cancer screening will be dictated by that price applied across 100 million adults over the age of 50 in the United States. The resultant price tag of about $100 billion a year would not be sustainable for the healthcare system of the U.S., or any other developed economy.

However, if Galleri ultimately charges payors $250 or so for an annual screening test, the market dynamics change completely. As explained in my prior piece, even if only a fraction of eligible adults take up MCED screening, I believe Grail could support a market capitalization of $10–20 billion.

The competitive landscape further strengthens Grail’s position. The barriers to entry in MCED are not trivial, as it would require both a considerable amount of capital and a substantial amount of time for another company to come up with datasets comparable to those of Grail’s extensive methylation-based data, multi-year clinical trial data, and, most importantly, the resultant FDA approval, if Galleri is approved by the FDA.

Public awareness and consumer acceptance of Galleri also remain difficult to assess. The overwhelming majority of Americans spend more than a thousand dollars a year insuring their cars, though the probability of a catastrophic car accident is significantly lower than the lifetime risk of cancer. Of course, rules and regulations play a larger role there, and no reasonable person expects the government to require us to go through an annual blood test. However, as awareness grows, it is not unreasonable to expect that a meaningful segment of adults—particularly those who are affluent, health-conscious, or concerned about family cancer history—will pay for Galleri even without reimbursement. It is entirely possible that the self-pay market for Galleri is likely depressed more by low awareness than by unwillingness to pay.

All of this underscores just how early we still are in building public awareness of MCED screening and in intelligently forecasting Galleri’s commercial prospects.

The Risks that Move Me to Downgrade to HOLD

As Mr. Market is now recognizing much of what was previously underappreciated in terms of the clinical promise and the regulatory status of Galleri that make Grail difficult to displace, the stock is no longer severely undervalued.

On balance, however, the disconnect between valuation and fundamentals that once justified buying has ceased to exist, and the substantially higher stock price now counsels caution. Specifically, the following risks are now more pronounced in light of the higher stock price:

First, FDA approval is not guaranteed.

Second, reimbursement at scale remains uncertain. The public healthcare system may adopt MCED testing much more slowly than the company expects as the nation stares at record budget deficits.

Third, competition will intensify as more companies seek to enter the space.

Fourth, various payors (other than affluent self-paying boomers) may push for significant price cuts. In any event, Galleri’s price tag will have to come down significantly over time in order not to break the bank of most consumers or the payors. Whether Grail can accommodate that by achieving the necessary economies of scale and operational efficiency is yet to be seen.

In short, these factors matter far more at a $100 share price than they did at around $50, and that is really the essential point of this piece.

On the other side of the same coin, however, it is important to acknowledge the new risks of inaction or selling at the current price. If FDA approval and payor adoption progress faster than expected, Grail’s stock could appreciate significantly from here. The company may also attract interest from large pharmaceutical companies, particularly those facing patent cliffs and the resulting revenue droughts, sooner than the market anticipates. Investors who choose not to add shares today or sell shares should at least recognize this potential opportunity cost.

My Approach as a Shareholder

For my part, I am holding a portion of the position I accumulated earlier this year. Given the extraordinary appreciation and the push-and-pull between bulls and bears that I described in the paragraph immediately above, I decided to harvest the majority of the fruits of my good luck and hard work. I am actively hunting for better opportunities where I can be more confident about the return/risk ratios than what Grail stock now represents. I would buy back the shares if the price declines substantially from here such that the return/risk ratio is better.

For investors who wish to capture some of upside without necessarily parting with their shares, selling out-of-the-money covered calls at a $100 or higher exercise price may be a reasonable strategy, though it requires the investors to be comfortable with the possibility that shares may be called away if and when the stock price exceeds the option exercise price.

Conclusion: A Strong Company, A Fair Valuation

Grail remains a strong company with a promising asset, a growing commercial footprint, and a presentable balance sheet (for an early-stage biotech company at least), all of which have further extended its runway for long-term growth. My downgrade to Hold reflects valuation discipline rather than any erosion of confidence. To my pleasant surprise, the stock has simply moved faster and farther than I anticipated when I first built my position. I will revisit this rating as new clinical, regulatory, and business developments unfold.

data.title: Why The Market Just Nuked IREN: This Selloff Makes No Sense
data.description:
Stock performance and previous coverage:

IREN Limited (IREN) isn’t in investors’ best graces, and not for good reason. The stock shed over 15% on Tuesday, marking the stock’s highest volume day ever, with 83.2 million shares traded. The stock seems to be recovering nicely in pre-market trading on Wednesday, up around 2.5% as investors buy the dip.

IREN has emerged as one of the names deeply tied to the AI story, a major shift from Bitcoin mining. This shift was one that investors liked, and that was before AI bubble concerns took over the market in the past month. IREN, along with the neocloud peers CoreWeave (CRWV), Applied Digital (APLD), and Nebius (NBIS), all had double-digit losses over the past month. IREN is even underperforming all peers, down 32.3%, except for CoreWeave, down 43%.

What I think happened here is that the market got ahead of itself and priced in the positives too soon for these players. When you look at the year-to-date and 6-month performance, where IREN is up 319% and 371% respectively, you get a different story.

I last covered IREN in early October, maintaining my Buy from early September. I’ll be honest, the timing of my October buy call wasn’t my finest, with the stock down around 35% since then. Still, this is exactly why the "buying in bunches" strategy works best for speculative AI plays that have volatility carved into their DNA. These names, as you can tell from one glance at the chart, never move in straight lines. When sentiment swings, the stock overshoots in both directions.

The fundamental setup hasn’t changed. Capacity is expanding, and AI-focused compute demand is getting tighter. So, the way I see it, the selloff has more to do with a broad market freak-out than anything company-specific. If you believed the long-term AI-compute buildout thesis in September, the current pullback towards the low $40s level looks far more like an opportunity than a red flag.

Investment thesis:

At the risk of repeating myself, the story here is still the same: the long-term value of this company will be determined by the pace of its AI Cloud transition, and while Bitcoin mining may remain the primary revenue driver today, I anticipate that the company’s shift into AI will come sooner than expected.

IREN’s pivot toward high-margin, contract-backed AI compute, like the deal we saw with Microsoft (MSFT) back in early November, is its next catalyst. Management’s aggressive revenue trajectory targets about $3.4 billion in annualized AI-cloud revenue by late 2026. Nearly $1.9 billion of that run rate is expected to come from the Microsoft partnership once it reaches full scale. That’s a big deal, and the market treated it as such, as the stock surged over 20% on that announcement alone, pushing it to a high of $75. I think that’s where the “hype” I mentioned comes in. The stock briefly traded as if IREN had already secured and deployed the entire GPU fleet and was operating at full AI-cloud utilization.

So, why did the market abandon IREN on Tuesday?

Before we get to Tuesday, there was Monday. That is where it all started.

Here’s the timeline:

On December 1, the company announced a $2B (upsizeable to $2.3B) convertible offering alongside an equity raise intended to retire its outstanding 2029 and 2030 notes.

By December 2, convertible-arbitrage flows took over, leading to a 50–80 million-share short position as legacy hedges were unwound, creating a purely mechanical selloff. The stock fell roughly 15% into the mid-$40s, with several billion dollars in volume clearing between the $45–$55 range. During the final trading hour, an additional 40-55 million shares were sold in concentrated bursts.

December 3 rolls around, and IREN prices the equity portion, 39.7 million shares at $41.12, raising $1.63 billion. At the same time, the company repurchased a combined $544 million of its 2029 and 2030 convertible notes through privately negotiated transactions. It also priced $1 billion of new 0.25% notes due 2032 and $1 billion of new 1.00% notes due 2033, with optional $150 million add-ons each.

My take:

I think this is a textbook balance sheet reset, the kind you typically see from a company preparing for its next phase of scale. In other words, not a distress move. By retiring its 2029 and 2030 convertible notes and replacing them with lower-cost, longer-maturity financing, IREN removed a major overhang that it would eventually have faced. So, while the equity raise created short-term pressure, to say the least, strategically, the company is now in a much stronger position. If anything, a cleaner balance sheet improves the probability that IREN can scale into its AI Cloud opportunity faster than the market is currently pricing in.

Valuation:

Compared to peers, IREN is the cheapest name on valuation. It trades at the lowest forward EV/Sales multiple, 10.1x, below CoreWeave at 10.7x, and dramatically below Applied Digital and Nebius, which sit at 29x and 43x, respectively. Price/Sales IREN is at 14x, compared to 37x for Applied Digital and more than 60x for Nebius. IREN’s forward EV/EBITDA of 15x is also materially lower than CoreWeave’s 17.5x and miles below Applied Digital’s 143x. Even on Price/Book, IREN looks cheaper than the rest.

On the technical level, I’d like to see RSI recover back into the 50 levels and for the stock to gain momentum and hold above $41. Until then, I suggest investors watch the technicals closely, as the fundamentals remain intact.

data.title: SHYL: Short Duration High Yield ETF
data.description:
Thesis

The Xtrackers Short Duration High Yield Bond ETF (SHYL) is a fixed income exchange traded fund we have not covered before. With an extended high yield market, we are going to analyze this name and articulate why shorter duration bonds are a better risk/reward in today's environment.

What does the fund do?

First, let us take a look at the ETF's objective:

Xtrackers Short Duration High Yield Bond ETF (the “Fund”) seeks investment results that correspond generally to the performance, before fees and expenses, of the Solactive USD High Yield Corporates Total Market 0-5 Year Index (the “Underlying Index”).

SHYL is therefore a passive ETF that aims to replicate the above Solactive Index. As per its factsheet (which investors can find via the provided link), the index:

The Solactive USD High Yield Corporates Total Market 0-5 Year Index is a rules-based, market value weighted Index designed to track the performance of High Yield rated corporate bonds issued in USD with 0 to 5 years term to maturity. The issuer’s domicile is not relevant. The Solactive USD High Yield Corporates Total Market 0-5 Year Index is a Total Return Index, i.e. coupon payments will be reinvested in the index on each Adjustment day.

As per the same fact-sheet we get an index which takes positions in very short dated high yield bonds:

This set-up results in an ETF with a very short duration of just 2.3 years. A short duration means the fund has less sensitivity to credit spreads and interest rates when compared to more traditional HY ETFs. This set-up is more conservative for a down market, but lags during bull set-ups.

Current fund composition

Let us take a look at how the ETF is currently set-up via its passive approach. From a geography stand-point the U.S. is the largest exposure:

  • U.S: 84%
  • Canada: 4.4%
  • UK: 2.2%
  • Japan: 1.6%
  • Others: residual

The ETF is overweight BB credits which make up the bulk of the holdings:

  • AAA: 2.2%
  • BBB: 1%
  • BB: 54%
  • B: 32%
  • CCC: 11%
  • other: residual

The fund does not take excessive risks, but does have a large CCC bucket of 11%. The short tenor however is a mitigant here. The fund is very granular, containing over 700 names, with the vast majority representing less than 1% of the holdings. Only the top-10 holdings represent more than 1% of the fund.

Other analytics for the name are as follows:

  • AUM: $0.24 bil
  • Expense ratio: 0.2%
  • Duration: 2.3 years
  • SEC yield: 6.6%
  • Annualized volatility: 5.4%

The fund has a low expense ratio, but it is a passive ETF thus as expected. The SEC yield is also low, but it is symptomatic of overextended markets in credit.

Performance versus regular HY

What sets this ETF apart is its low volatility stemming from a low duration. We are going to compare the name versus a traditional HY fund and a peer:

The names we are using here for charting purposes are the State Street SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK) and the State Street SPDR Bloomberg Short Term High Yield Bond ETF (SJNK). We can see that SHYL and SJNK have a very similar total return profile, while JNK lags.

What is very helpful for a reader is to look at what happened in 2022, a bear market for credit: SHYL had only half the drawdown when compared to JNK, and recovered fairly quickly. JNK has yet to catch up with SHYL when looking at a total return graph.

Being long shorter duration high yield during a bear market is a more conservative stance, and really helps in the long term when looking at drawdowns and total returns. It also offers more flexibility in adjusting positions.

Credit spreads are too low for us

At this juncture of the macro cycle, we view high-yield corporate credit as significantly overextended:

If we look at the ICE BofA Single-B US High Yield Index Option Adjusted Spreads, we can see they are at the bottom of the 10-year historic range. This simply means the next move is going to be up. We do not know when, but we would not want to buy HY here. Wait for a widening in spreads.

Does this mean an investor should own zero HY? Not really. Does it mean one can be smart about allocating exposure to shorter dated HY? We think so. There are always ways to adjust exposure per market conditions, and today's market screams of overextension. We much prefer a fund like SHYL now than outright JNK. As we saw from the historical charts one can expect a much narrower drawdown for SHYL during a risk-off environment.

Conclusion

SHYL is a fixed income ETF. The fund focuses on shorter dated HY names, and follows a passive approach. The ETF had a very shallow drawdown in 2022, and will perform much better than JNK during a recession. With credit spreads too tight we favor a rotation from JNK into SHYL.

data.title: Dollar Tree: A Surprising K-Shaped Economy Winner
data.description:
Brace yourself for another “K-shaped economy” article. Dollar Tree (DLTR) issued a strong Q3 earnings report on Wednesday, Dec. 3, posting a double beat and guidance raise. Shares traded up modestly in the moments after the data hit. Whether strong numbers from this retailer that caters to low-end income households are always tough to suss out.

Interestingly, DLTR has been a stellar 2025 performer. Shares are up by almost 50% year-to-date, outpacing the S&P 500 by almost 30 percentage points. The $22 billion market cap stock has also handily outpaced the equal-weight Consumer Discretionary and Consumer Staples ETFs in 2025.

Zoom out, and returns are not as appealing. I had a hold rating on DLTR back in November 2022. Over the past three-plus years, shares are down 27% compared to the 70% S&P 500 ascent. Today, I’m sticking with a "Hold" on DLTR. I’ll offer a refreshed valuation and note key technical levels to monitor.

DLTR a 2025 Retail Winner

In December, Dollar Tree reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q3 non-GAAP EPS of $1.21 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $1.08, while revenue of $4.75 billion, up 9.4% from the same period last year, was a modest $60 million beat. The company hiked its full-year fiscal 2025 adjusted EBITDA from continuing operations range to $5.60-$5.80 in light of new data and significant stock buybacks.

Shares traded up by 1.7% shortly after the numbers hit the tape, which was a much better reaction compared to the pair of previous earnings days. In June and September, the stock plunged 8.4%. The options market was indeed braced for a big move—8.1%, according to the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the December 3 report.

Looking back on the quarter that was, Dollar Tree’s same-store sales growth was not only a relief but also impressive. The near-double-digit YoY jump certainly included some goods inflation, but it was clear that consumers showed up over the August through October period (of course, we don’t yet know how its holiday-shopping season is going).

Still, higher traffic more than offset a slightly lower average ticket price. Moreover, the management team proved its prowess, evidenced by a 10.8% gross profit increase compared to Q3 2024. The gross margin, key for retailers, ticked up by 40 basis points—a likely driver of the positive share-price reaction.

The firm noted success with its multi-price strategy, and that was apparent in a more favorable product sales mix in Q3. Halloween sales were healthy, while 106 new stores were opened over the fall period. For stockholders, DLTR has been a buyer of its shares, and there remains $2.0 billion available in its repurchase program.

Back to its new strategy—Dollar Tree’s recent Investor Day in October laid out a multi‑year plan to expand and modernize the chain around its multi‑price strategy. At the event, the company projected a 12%–15% EPS CAGR target from fiscal 2027–2029, driven by 3%–4% comp growth, 5%–7% revenue growth, and roughly 325 net new stores per year. Management highlighted a “test and learn” culture and outlined operational initiatives, including improving underperforming stores with thousands of refreshes and renovations. So far, so good.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be about flat in fiscal 2025, though that could get taken higher once the sellside updates its numbers in light of today’s report. Out-year non-GAAP per-share earnings could reach $6.4, with mid-teens growth assumed. Further solid gains are expected beyond that. But analysts have been lukewarm on DLTR, as seen by 14 EPS upgrades over the past 90 days compared to 10 downgrades.

Finally, while overall profitability trends are middle of the road, I’d call out that free cash flow per share is very high at $9.14. The resulting FCF yield is almost 10%.

Dollar Tree: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, if we assume a normalized earnings per share of $6 and apply a 19x price-to-earnings multiple, then shares should trade near $114. With the stock at $112 after the Q3 report, that puts shares close to fair value. DLTR is also not a bargain on a price-to-sales basis. A 1.4x PEG is fair, in my view, and aligns with the guidance offered at the Investor Day.

DLTR: Mixed Valuation Metrics

Key risks for Dollar Tree include increased competition within the low-end retail space. Walmart (WMT) and Dollar General (DG) are just two of many stalwart firms that aim to take share from DLTR. Of course, a macro slowdown could negatively impact consumer spending.

It’s always a mixed picture whether a weaker consumer is good or bad for dollar stores, but we’ve seen that there can be a further trade-down to food banks if conditions deteriorate further. Food price volatility and labor costs are other potential threats.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a projected Q4 2025 earnings date of Wednesday, March 25, BMO. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares close to fair value and strong execution by the management team, DLTR’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that the stock is stuck between support and resistance. I do like that the rising long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. What’s more, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graphs has recovered into a neutral to slightly bullish zone.

But take a look at the volume by price profile on the left side of the chart. It’s clear that there’s a large amount of shares traded all the way up to $150. That's a significant congestion zone that will make further price gains tough on the bulls.

For now, I see support between $75 and $80, with another layer just below $100, while resistance is likely close to the $120 mark.

DLTR: $120 Resistance, Rising 200dma, $98 Support

The Bottom Line

I have a "Hold" rating on Dollar Tree. The valuation appears fair to me, while strategy execution appears solid (with share buybacks). The chart, meanwhile, is muddled. A rally through $120 would be bullish.

data.title: Lexicon Pharmaceuticals Has Multiple Paths To Upside, But Formidable Hurdles As Well
data.description:
The Lexicon Pharmaceuticals (LXRX) story has had quite a few twists and turns over the years, but arguably the one constant has been that this tiny biotech can produce drugs that deliver encouraging efficacy in the clinic, but can’t get them across the finish line from a regulatory and commercial perspective.

Up about 20% since my last update, some positive developments on the development of sotagliflozin (Zynquista) for Type 1 diabetes has rejuvenated sentiment to some extent, though the share price has lagged the broader biotech segment over that time.

Heading into 2026, Lexicon has two potential commercial opportunities in relatively advanced stages and a third heading toward Phase III - any one of which could conceivably support a share price well above today’s level. That does coax me to a "Buy" stance now, but each of these opportunities has its own particular clinical, regulatory, and commercial challenges that have to be factored into the company’s outlook. At best, this is a highly risky biotech long shot that could see multiple news events in 2026 dramatically impact the share price for good or bad.

Zynquista – It’s Not Dead Yet

Like the main character from a slasher movie franchise, Zynquista, the formulation of sotagliflozin that Lexicon has been developing for diabetes, gets buried only to rise again, with the latest revival coming back in the fall when the FDA agreed to reengage with the company on a possible route to approval in Type 1 diabetes.

Management has been working on Zynquista as an oral therapy for insufficiently-controlled Type 1 diabetes for over a decade. While the drug has consistently shown meaningful efficacy in this indication (the InTandem studies), the FDA has equally consistently thrown up roadblocks related to the risk of diabetic ketoacidosis (or DKA), a rare but potentially very serious side-effect that can occur more frequently with medications like Zynquista.

Despite various logical proposed mediation efforts, the FDA has at times effectively put the company in the position of trying to “prove a negative” with respect to DKA risk and there have been repeated back-and-forth interactions over the years, including another complete response letter (or “rejection”) a year ago.

Once again, though, there is a ray of hope. Management’s characterization of meetings between the company and the FDA suggests that the FDA is willing to accept data from a third-party investigator-initiated study with respect to the DKA risk of Zynquista (as well as data on cardiovascular risk reduction, kidney function, and other efficacy and safety metrics).

This study, the STENO study, is being funded by the Novo Foundation and is enrolling 2,000 “high risk” people with Type 1 diabetes in an open-label study of sotagliflozin, semaglutide, and finerenone (and various combos) to see whether these drugs result in better glucose control and improved outcomes (including cardiac events, kidney disease, and overall mortality). Lexicon management has claimed that this satisfies what the FDA has been asking for for years now and that it could serve as the basis for a resubmission to the FDA in early 2026 and possibly approval before year-end.

I believe the risk of adverse clinical outcomes is low; there’s plenty of safety data on the drug already, even if not in the form or study design that the FDA wanted. I do still see risk on the commercial side.

On the positive side, Lexicon would be the only company with an FDA-approved and labeled drug in the SGLT class for T1 diabetes (sotagliflozin is technically a dual SGLT-1/-2 inhibitor, while other drugs like Lilly’s (LLY) Jardiance and AstraZeneca’s (AZN) Farxiga are SGLT-2 inhibitors, but the clinical significance of that distinction has never really been established). That alone could create some exciting pricing opportunities versus how SGLT-2 drugs are presently reimbursed, and I do think there is pent-up demand for solutions like Zynquista. With somewhere around 1.3M to 1.4M people with inadequately-controlled Type 1 diabetes in the U.S., that’s meaningful.

On the negative side, only about half of people with T1D see an endocrinologist regularly, and most manage their disease with/through a family practitioner. That’s simply too much real estate for a company of Lexicon’s size to address, and so a partner would be vital even if the company were to assemble its own endocrinology-focused sales team.

There are also risk and concerns regarding reimbursement and competition. While Lexicon’s SGLT-2 rivals cannot market for Type 1 diabetes, doctors can prescribe those drugs for Type 1 diabetes and have been doing so for years. Reimbursement is often tough to get due to the lack of labeling, but it’s at least plausible that insurers could become more open to paying for SGLT-2 drugs for this indication (particularly generics) over time, and especially so if one of the SGLT-2 players decided to sponsor a study confirming safety and efficacy. Said differently, there’s a non-zero risk that Lexicon may not enjoy as long a period of advantageous pricing as bulls may hope.

Inpefa Could Get A Second Lease On Life

Zynquista is not Lexicon’s only play with sotagliflozin. As Inpefa, the drug is approved for heart failure, though the company failed in its attempts to find a commercial partner and then to market the drug on its own with a hospital-focused sales force.

While the heart failure opportunity in the U.S. is basically in stasis (the drug is still on the market and the company has introduced a "virtual sales support system" to support commercial efforts, but it generates little revenue), the company is pursuing a label indication for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, a condition that affects over 1M people in the United States. Although Bristol-Myers (BMY) did get approval for Cazymos in 2022 for obstructive HCM, the drug has serious side effects and it is safe to say that HCM is a serious condition for which safe and effective treatments are sorely lacking (and needed).

Management has been accelerating enrollment in its Phase III SONATA study in HCM and believes they may complete enrollment in Q1’26, setting up a data read-out likely in Q1’27. Given the biological rationale at play here, I expect sotagliflozin/Inpefa to work here, but commercialization remains a key question.

Management has said they would look to commercialize the drug themselves, leveraging that virtual sales model. While the lack of direct on-label competition and the more concentrated nature of treatment (most HCM patients are treated at large medical centers) would make this more viable, I have my doubts about Lexicon’s ability to execute on a nontraditional solo commercialization strategy. I believe that, at a minimum, the company would have to raise capital to rebuild at least some of the infrastructure it tore down when it decided to pivot back to a focus on R&D.

Pilavapadin Needs Clear Path To Success

Last and not least is pilavapadin, Lexicon’s novel AAK1 inhibitor for pain. After somewhat successful Phase II results, the company has submitted an end-of-phase II meeting request with the FDA and hopes to start a Phase III study next year, while also “engaging with potential partners”.

The issue here is that the Phase II studies in diabetic neuropathy (RELIEF and PROGRESS) did not show particularly impressive placebo-adjusted improvements in average daily pain scores, with 0.67 and 0.43, respectively, at the best dose. With established generic alternatives like duloxetine and pregabalin/gabapentin delivering 0.80 to 1.2 placebo-adjusted improvements at similar development stages, it’s an open question as to whether pilavapadin is good enough to carve out a commercial niche against these cheaper generics.

I’m going to try to offer the “steel man” argument here. First, there was an unusually high placebo response rate in PROGRESS that may have undermined the apparent efficacy of the drug and it’s possible that a Phase III study won’t show similar “noise”. Second, it is possible that the drug may distinguish itself on tolerability – real-world experience with duloxetine and pregabalin hasn’t matched what was seen in clinical studies largely because many patients find the side effects (nausea, somnolence, dizziness, et al) to be intolerable. Likewise, there are some patients who simply don’t respond.

The combination of tolerability and a different mechanism of action means that even if the final efficacy data don’t look as impressive, there could still be a path to commercial differentiation for pilavapadin. Basically, if this drug produces “adequate” pain relief but fewer side effects and/or works in patients that don’t even get adequate relief with other drugs, there is a path open to at least hundreds of millions of dollars in revenue.

As is the case with Zynquista, and I would argue Inpefa as well, commercial success will almost certainly require a partner – I just don’t see Lexicon having the capacity to maximize the potential of this drug on their own without a stunning improvement in efficacy in Phase III.

The Outlook

Given Lexicon's current R&D focus, its quarterly financial reports aren't all that interesting or significant at this point. Nevertheless, third quarter results saw the company book about $14M in revenue, with over $13M coming from its Novo Nordisk licensing deal (another $30M will unlock when LX9851 moves into human studies), about $19M in R&D costs and an operating loss of about $12M.

Lexicon ended the quarter with $145M in cash (including $29M in restricted cash). With only about $51M in negative operating cash flow year-to-date, that actually suggests a pretty decent runway for Lexicon's cash balance, though running a Phase III trial for pilavapadin will increase the burn. At a minimum, there should be enough cash to see a yes/no decision from the FDA on Zynquista and likely get to the SONATA Phase III results in early 2027.

Across all three drugs/formulations that Lexicon has in more advanced development, each addresses a market of 1M or more potential users and with a possibility for on-label differentiation supporting attractive reimbursement. If just one of those drugs has a line of sight to $500M in peak annual revenue, the going rate for commercial-stage biotech (4.5x to 5.5x peak sales estimates) would support a fair value of about $6 - $7.50 per share.

This also excludes the possibilities and potential of LX9851, a preclinical ACSL5 that Lexicon licensed to Novo Nordisk (NVO) and that should enter human studies in 2026.

There are clearly still multiple meaningful potential stumbling blocks. The FDA may still find the DKA risk too high to approve Zynquista for T1D, Inpefa may be less effective than I hope in HCM, and pilavapadin may not be effective enough to have a real commercial future. Likewise, even if any or all of these drugs make it past the FDA approval phase, there are meaningful questions about Lexicon’s ability to commercialize them and attain whatever theoretical peak revenue may be out there.

In short, that’s why a company with multiple “$6/share drugs” trades for less than $1.50 today. I'd also note that there's not much coverage here, but volume is still relatively strong (over 2M shares traded daily, on average).

The Bottom Line

Relative to Lexicon’s history, I have more confidence in this management team, though I do think their assertion that they can effectively market any of these drugs on their own is likely optimistic. Time will tell, and of course it is true that other biotechs have successfully marketed “specialty” drugs on their own despite modest resources (and again, the failures of prior managements do not automatically transfer to this management’s shoulders).

At this point, Lexicon remains an interesting speculation, and one with some meaningful upcoming potential catalysts in 2026.While I’m hesitant to get too positive, particularly in light of my concerns about commercialization and/or finding good commercial partners, there are late-stage opportunities here that even as long shots could still pan out.
